claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let me ask about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

1. **How old is your child?** (If you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can go through each of them)

2. **What is your child's name?** (This helps me keep track if you have multiple children)

The age of the child is important because:
- Children under 16 may automatically qualify
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over generally don't qualify

Please share these details so we can proceed with your eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'd like to find out about Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me continue with the assessment for Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news so far! Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), they fall into the automatic qualification age category.

Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

For an 8-year-old, this would typically mean they are not in paid work at all, which is normal for a child of this age. Can you confirm that Alex is not working 24 or more hours per week in paid employment?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's only 8, so he's just at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that's what I expected for an 8-year-old at school.

Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means - is Alex receiving any benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name? 

For an 8-year-old, this would be very unusual. Most children this age would not be claiming benefits in their own right. Can you confirm that Alex is not receiving any such benefits in his own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now the next check:

**Is Alex in any form of apprenticeship?**

For context, certain types of apprenticeships can disqualify a child from Child Benefit, though this varies by region:
- In England: all apprenticeships disqualify
- In Scotland: Modern Apprenticeships disqualify  
- In Wales: most apprenticeships disqualify except Foundation Apprenticeships

However, since Alex is only 8 years old and at school, I expect he's not in an apprenticeship. Can you confirm that Alex is NOT in any apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any apprenticeship - he's just 8 and at primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Alex meets all the child-related requirements so far. Now I need to check your eligibility as the claimant.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, OR
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold), OR
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days), OR
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself, OR
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources, OR
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national, OR
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these situations apply to you?** The most common is simply that you normally live in the UK. Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! You meet the residency requirements as you live in the UK.

Now, the next question:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can show responsibility if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex**, OR
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would provide for food, clothes, housing, pocket money, presents, etc.), OR
3. You pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement

**Which of these applies to you?** Do you live with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! You clearly have responsibility for Alex as you live together.

Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. This is important to know because:
- If no one else is claiming, we can proceed
- If someone else is claiming but you both agree that you should claim instead, we can proceed
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC will need to make the decision

**Is anyone else (such as Alex's other parent or another family member) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current living arrangement?**

1. **Alex is in hospital**
2. **Alex is in care** (looked after by the local authority)
3. **Alex is fostered** (living with foster parents)
4. **Alex is living normally at home** with you

Based on what you've told me (that Alex lives at home with you and goes to primary school), I expect it's option 4 - living normally at home. Can you confirm which situation applies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually Alex has been in care with the local authority for a couple of months now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see, thank you for clarifying that. This is an important detail as it affects eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Since Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months, I need to ask some specific questions about this:

**Does either of the following apply:**

1. **Has Alex been in care for 8 weeks or less?** (You mentioned a couple of months, which would be more than 8 weeks), OR

2. **Does Alex spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you?**

For context:
- Child Benefit can continue for up to 8 weeks when a child goes into care
- You can also continue claiming if the child regularly spends 24+ hours per week at home with you
- You can claim again if the child spends 2 consecutive nights per week or 7+ days in a row at home

Since you said "a couple of months," that sounds like it's been more than 8 weeks. **Does Alex come home to spend at least 24 hours per week with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Let me think... it's been about 7 weeks actually, so yes, it's within that 8-week period.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, so it's within the 8-week period. Now I have one final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local authority covering the costs of Alex's care while he's in their care?

Generally, you cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child's accommodation or maintenance. Can you tell me - is the local council paying for Alex's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Alex's care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex! ✓**

Based on the information you've provided, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

### **Why You're Eligible:**

✓ Alex is 8 years old (under 16 - automatically qualifying)  
✓ Alex is not in paid work  
✓ Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right  
✓ Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship  
✓ You live in the UK (Manchester)  
✓ You have responsibility for Alex (living with him)  
✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex  
✓ **Alex has been in care for 7 weeks (within the 8-week grace period)**  
✓ The local council is not paying for Alex's care  

### **Next Steps - How to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### **Important Note About the Care Situation:**

Since Alex is in care and you're within the 8-week period, please be aware that if Alex remains in care beyond 8 weeks total AND doesn't spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you, your Child Benefit entitlement would stop at that point.

**You can apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about claiming or your eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16 - automatically qualifying age). Alex is not in paid work 24+ hours per week. Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right (ESA or Universal Credit). Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) meeting residency requirements. The claimant lives with Alex, establishing responsibility. No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex has been in local authority care for 7 weeks (within the 8-week allowable period). The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child Alex. Despite Alex being in local authority care, the claim remains valid because it's within the 8-week grace period and the council is not paying for Alex's care.

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit

Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16 - automatically qualifying age). Alex is not in paid work 24+ hours per week. Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right (ESA or Universal Credit). Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) meeting residency requirements. The claimant lives with Alex, establishing responsibility. No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex has been in local authority care for 7 weeks (within the 8-week allowable period). The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child Alex. Despite Alex being in local authority care, the claim remains valid because it's within the 8-week grace period and the council is not paying for Alex's care.
